does: This position paper argues for precise definitional boundaries in machine unlearning research by proposing that the term should be reserved exclusively for dataset-defined deletion—removing the training influence of a precisely specified forget set such that the model becomes approximately indistinguishable from one retrained without that data. The authors contend that many current LLM research tasks labeled as "unlearning" including refusal behaviors, entity removal, knowledge suppression, and targeted behavioral changes actually pursue fundamentally different objectives that are policy-dependent rather than data-deletion objectives. They demonstrate that this terminological confusion creates serious practical problems: papers make different implicit guarantees under identical labels, metrics designed for one task get misapplied to others, and benchmarks reward surface-level approaches like low ROUGE scores rather than genuine influence removal, ultimately obscuring whether methods actually achieve their stated goals.
connects: This work responds to rapidly expanding research on machine unlearning in large language models, which has grown as regulatory pressure (right to be forgotten), copyright concerns, and safety requirements have intensified. The paper builds on prior taxonomies of model editing and alignment but pushes back against loose application of unlearning terminology across distinct problems. It connects to foundational work on machine unlearning from federated learning and privacy literature that established rigorous definitions based on retraining baselines, while acknowledging that LLM-specific work has diluted these definitions. The position engages with concurrent efforts to formalize alignment, knowledge editing, and refusal mechanisms, recognizing these as separate challenges that require distinct methodological approaches rather than unified treatment under the unlearning umbrella.
enables: This clarification work opens space for more rigorous future research by establishing proper terminology and evaluation frameworks for different deletion and suppression objectives in LLMs. It enables development of task-specific benchmarks and baselines—such as those appropriate for alignment versus data influence removal versus knowledge editing—that can meaningfully evaluate whether methods achieve their actual goals rather than gaming surface metrics. By distinguishing dataset-defined deletion from policy-dependent objectives, the position enables researchers to select appropriate baselines and guarantees for their specific problem, potentially leading to safer and more auditable LLM modification techniques. It points toward future work that develops principled evaluation methods for each category and clarifies what technical and safety guarantees different objectives can actually provide, ultimately supporting more reliable approaches to model updating and control.
